plain_language_summary: Effective January 1, 2026, the Colorado standard minimum wage is $15.16 per hour under COMPS Order #42. Some local minimums (e.g., Denver at $19.29, Boulder at $16.82) are higher and take precedence. Tipped employees have a separate base wage ($12.14); see co_labor_002.
required_actions: Display the current COMPS Order poster at every work site
Update payroll rates each January 1 to the new state and applicable local minimums
Apply the higher of state or local minimum where the work is performed
prohibited_actions: Paying below the state minimum
Skipping the annual increase posted by CDLE

source.title: Colorado Overtime and Minimum Pay Standards (COMPS) Order #42 and 2026 PAY CALC Order
source.publisher: Colorado Department of Labor and Employment (CDLE), Division of Labor Standards and Statistics

plain_language_summary: Effective January 1, 2026, the Colorado tipped minimum wage is $12.14 per hour (reflecting a $3.02 tip credit) under COMPS Order #42. Tips received plus the cash wage must equal or exceed the full state minimum ($15.16) for every workweek. If tips do not bring the employee to full minimum, the employer must make up the difference. Colorado follows the federal 80/20/30 rule for non-tipped work.
required_actions: Track tipped vs. non-tipped time
Reconcile each pay period to verify base + tips ≥ state minimum
Record any tip credit shortfall and pay it
prohibited_actions: Counting non-tipped side work above the 80/20/30 threshold against the tip credit
Forcing tipped staff to share tips with managers, owners, or back-of-house in a manager-controlled pool
allowed_actions: Voluntary tip pooling among directly tipped employees

source.title: COMPS Order #42, Rule 3.4 (Tipped Employees) and federal 29 CFR 531.50–531.59

notes: Verified for 2026: $12.14/hr base cash wage for tipped employees.

plain_language_summary: Hours worked over 40 in a workweek must be paid at 1.5× the regular rate. The workweek is a fixed, recurring 168-hour period the employer designates. For 2026, the salary threshold for exemption is $57,784 per year ($1,111.23 per week).
required_actions: Define and post the workweek
Pay 1.5× for all hours over 40 per workweek to non-exempt employees
prohibited_actions: Averaging hours across two workweeks to avoid OT
Paying flat shifts to non-exempt employees
exceptions: Bona-fide exempt salaried employees meeting the duties test and 2026 salary threshold ($57,784)

source.title: COMPS Order #42, Rule 4 (Overtime) and FLSA 29 USC 207

plain_language_summary: Hours worked over 12 in a workday — OR over 12 consecutive hours regardless of when the workday starts — must be paid at 1.5×. Daily OT and weekly OT are calculated separately; the employee gets the greater of the two but never both for the same hour. See lib/breaks.ts daily-OT helper for code-level reference.
required_actions: Track daily hours per shift
Pay 1.5× for hours 13+ in a workday
Compare daily-OT total vs. weekly-OT total per pay period; pay the higher
prohibited_actions: Treating split shifts as separate days to avoid daily OT when the consecutive-12-hour rule applies

plain_language_summary: Employees scheduled to work shifts longer than 5 hours are entitled to a 30-minute uninterrupted, duty-free meal period that begins between the 1st and 5th hour. The meal period is unpaid only if the employee is fully relieved of duty. If the employee cannot leave the work area or must respond to calls, the meal must be paid as on-duty time.
required_actions: Schedule the 30-min meal period to start within the first 5 hours of a 5+ hour shift
Document on-duty meal periods and pay them
prohibited_actions: Counting a meal period as unpaid when the employee was not fully relieved
Skipping the meal period without documented operational necessity and on-duty pay
allowed_actions: Mutual written agreement to take an on-duty paid meal period when the nature of work prevents full relief

plain_language_summary: Employees are entitled to a 10-minute paid rest period for every 4 hours worked or major fraction thereof. Rest periods cannot be combined with the meal period. If an employer fails to authorize a required rest period, the employee is owed an additional 10 minutes of wages for each missed break.
required_actions: Schedule rest periods as close to the midpoint of each 4-hour segment as practical
Pay 10 minutes of extra wages for each missed rest period
prohibited_actions: Replacing rest periods with extra meal time
Routinely waiving rest periods for staffing reasons
notes: Codified in lib/breaks.ts. Penalty for missed breaks verified in 2026 Wage Protection Rules.

plain_language_summary: Under the Healthy Families and Workplaces Act (HFWA), employees accrue 1 hour of paid sick leave per 30 hours worked, up to 48 hours per year. In 2026, pay for HFWA leave must be the 'same wages' the employee would have earned, excluding bonuses and OT premiums. For variable roles, use the role-specific rate or a 30-day lookback average.
required_actions: Track accrual at 1:30 ratio
Allow carryover of up to 48 hours into the next year
Maintain records of accrued, used, and available leave for 3 years
Calculate pay based on the rate for the missed shift (or 30-day average)
prohibited_actions: Disciplining or terminating an employee for using accrued HFWA leave
Requiring shift coverage as a precondition to use HFWA leave
allowed_actions: Front-loading 48 hours at the start of the year in lieu of accrual tracking

source.title: Colorado Healthy Families and Workplaces Act (CRS 8-13.3-401 et seq.) and 2026 Wage Protection Rules

notes: Codified in lib/paidSickLeave.ts. Pay calculation clarified in Feb 2026 rules.

plain_language_summary: Tips are the property of the employee who received them. Mandatory tip pooling is permitted only among directly tipped employees. In 2026, local governments with higher minimum wages may authorize a tip credit higher than the state's $3.02, provided the base wage is at least $12.14 (HB 25-1208). Service charges are not tips and must be clearly disclosed in writing if retained by the employer.
required_actions: Document any mandatory tip pool arrangement in writing
Distinguish service charges from tips on receipts; disclose retention policy in writing
Ensure base cash wage is at least $12.14 regardless of local tip credit
prohibited_actions: Manager or owner participation in tip pools
Paying employees from a tip pool for work performed when the restaurant is closed (e.g., morning prep)
Retaliating against employees who report tip violations
allowed_actions: Voluntary tip-sharing initiated by employees
Mandatory pools among directly tipped employees with documented allocation
notes: HB 25-1208 local tip credit flexibility verified for 2026.

plain_language_summary: Colorado employers must display several state and federal posters in a location accessible to all employees. For 2026, posters must reflect the $15.16 minimum wage and the $57,784 exempt salary threshold. A 'Notice of Paydays' with a mandatory QR code is also required.
required_actions: Display the 2026 COMPS Order poster
Display the HFWA paid sick leave poster
Display the Colorado workers' compensation poster
Display the Notice of Paydays (with QR code)
Display federal posters: FLSA, FMLA, EEO, OSHA, USERRA, IRCA
prohibited_actions: Hiding posters in manager-only areas
Using posters more than 12 months out of date
notes: Refresh annually; 2026 posters include QR codes and new thresholds.

plain_language_summary: Employees under 18 cannot handle alcoholic beverages. Employees 18-20 can serve alcohol with a meal at the table but cannot tend bar. For 2026, the youth minimum wage for non-emancipated minors is $12.89 per hour (85% of state min). Employers must maintain records to prove non-emancipated status.
required_actions: Verify age before assigning any alcohol-related duty
Restrict bar tending to 21+
Maintain records of non-emancipated status for employees paid the youth rate
Restrict minors from duties involving explosives/ammunition
prohibited_actions: Allowing under-18 employees to deliver, refill, or clear alcoholic beverages
Allowing 18-20 employees behind the bar
allowed_actions: Paying non-emancipated minors $12.89/hr (85% of standard min)

source.title: CRS 44-3-901; 2026 Colorado Youth Employment Opportunity Act updates

plain_language_summary: If the employer terminates the employee, all wages and compensation are due immediately. If the employee resigns, final pay is due on the next regular payday. Starting July 2026, the DLSS can adjudicate wage claims up to $13,000. Names of willful violators will be published on a public 'Wall of Shame'.
required_actions: Issue final paycheck immediately on involuntary termination
Pay all earned and unused vacation at termination
Issue final paycheck on next regular payday for voluntary resignation
prohibited_actions: Withholding final pay pending return of company property
Deducting from final pay without written authorization

source.title: Colorado Wage Act, CRS 8-4-109; 2026 DLSS updates

notes: Adjudication limit increased to $13k in July 2026.

plain_language_summary: Under the Colorado Equal Pay for Equal Work Act, all job postings must include the compensation range, bonus opportunities, commission structures, promotion timelines, and a benefits description. Post-selection notice must be given to current employees. Pay history cannot be used to set pay.
required_actions: Include salary, bonuses, commissions, and promotion timelines on job postings
Notify all employees of internal promotion opportunities the day they open
Notify current employees of the selected candidate for a position
prohibited_actions: Asking applicants for prior pay history
Setting starting pay based on prior pay history
Posting jobs without full compensation transparency

source.title: Colorado Equal Pay for Equal Work Act, CRS 8-5-201 et seq.; 2025/2026 amendments

notes: Transparency requirements expanded in 2026 to include bonuses and promotion timelines.

plain_language_summary: Selling, serving, or providing alcohol to anyone under 21 is a misdemeanor and a license violation. Reasonable reliance on a valid government-issued ID (physical or the myColorado™ app Digital ID) can be a defense. Establishments must not permit 21+ patrons to relay drinks to minors.
required_actions: Check ID for any patron who appears under 30
Accept valid physical IDs or the myColorado™ app Digital ID
Decline service if any doubt remains after ID review
Maintain incident logs for refusals and confiscated IDs
prohibited_actions: Serving anyone under 21 regardless of accompanying adult
Allowing a 21+ patron to relay drinks to a minor
Confiscating an ID and returning it after service
allowed_actions: Refusing service in good faith
Asking for a second form of ID
Requesting a physical ID if a digital ID is suspected fraudulent

plain_language_summary: Acceptable IDs in 2026 include: physical driver's licenses/IDs from any US state, US passports/cards, US military IDs, Colorado-issued tribal IDs, and the Colorado Digital ID™ via the myColorado™ app. Foreign passports are accepted at licensee discretion. Vertical (under-21) IDs require careful date math.
required_actions: Verify the ID is unaltered, the photo matches, and the birthdate is valid
For Digital ID, verify the floating hologram in the myColorado™ app
Decline if any element fails inspection
prohibited_actions: Accepting expired IDs
Accepting screenshots or photos of IDs
Taking physical possession of a customer's phone to view a digital ID
allowed_actions: Asking for a physical ID if digital ID verification is inconclusive
Calling a manager for review
notes: Digital ID manual verification process (hologram) verified.

plain_language_summary: Serving alcohol to a visibly intoxicated person is prohibited and triggers Dram Shop liability (capped at ~$460k-$480k in 2026). Indicators include slurred speech, impaired motor control, and aggressive behavior. Licensees must not allow visibly intoxicated persons to remain on premises without a ride-home plan.
required_actions: Cut off service when any visible-intoxication indicator is present
Offer water and food; facilitate a safe ride home
Document the refusal in the incident log
Notify the manager
prohibited_actions: Serving 'one more' to an intoxicated patron
Allowing a companion to order for an intoxicated patron
Permitting an intoxicated person to remain on-site without an acceptable purpose (e.g., waiting for ride)
allowed_actions: Refusing service in good faith
Calling EMS if the patron appears medically impaired

plain_language_summary: Alcohol service and consumption must occur within the licensed boundary. Patrons cannot leave the premises with open containers. Ejections and boundary control are the responsibility of the licensee and security staff.
required_actions: Train staff on the licensed boundary
Stop drink service before patrons cross the boundary
Maintain orderliness at the boundary and within the premises
prohibited_actions: Allowing patrons to walk drinks outside the licensed area
Selling alcohol to-go without a valid retail/delivery permit
allowed_actions: Properly licensed sealed-container retail sales (where applicable)

plain_language_summary: The Responsible Vendor Program (RVP) can mitigate penalties for violations. Staff must complete an approved course within 90 days of hire. Training must be renewed every two years. RVP certification is a critical defense in administrative hearings.
required_actions: Enroll new staff in RVP course within 90 days of hire
Retain training certificates on-site
Re-certify all staff every two years
allowed_actions: Use Responsible Vendor designation as a mitigation factor in administrative hearings

plain_language_summary: Licensees can refuse service for lawful reasons (intoxication, fake ID, conduct) but cannot discriminate based on protected classes under the Colorado Anti-Discrimination Act. Refusals should be consistent and documented to avoid claims of bias.
required_actions: Refuse service for any legal non-discriminatory reason
Document the reason for refusal in the incident log
Train staff on anti-discrimination laws
prohibited_actions: Refusing service based on race, religion, sex, orientation, gender identity, or disability
allowed_actions: Refusing service for violation of posted dress code or conduct rules

source.title: CRS 24-34-601; CDOR Liquor Enforcement Division guidance

plain_language_summary: Under CRS 44-3-801, licensees are liable for damages caused by a patron if they willfully and knowingly served a visibly intoxicated person or a minor. Claims must be filed within one year. Liability for noneconomic damages is capped statutory (inflation-adjusted, ~$460k-$480k in 2026).
required_actions: Maintain RVP certification to mitigate risk
Maintain incident logs of all refusals and ejections
Maintain liability insurance coverage
prohibited_actions: Willfully serving alcohol to a minor or intoxicated person

source.title: CRS 44-3-801; 2026 Secretary of State inflation adjustments

notes: One-year statute of limitations and 2026 caps verified.

plain_language_summary: Effective August 1, 2026, security personnel must hold a statewide license from the State Board of Private Security Services (HB 25-1262). Use of force is limited to reasonable de-escalation or defense of self/others from imminent force. Excessive force triggers both criminal and license liability.
required_actions: Ensure all security staff hold a valid state license (by Aug 2026)
Verify background checks and training for all security hires
Document every use of force in a dedicated incident report
prohibited_actions: Using excessive force during ejections
Carrying weapons or non-lethal tools without a specific board endorsement
allowed_actions: Using reasonable force to defend self or others
Using reasonable force to eject a trespasser after a refusal to leave
exceptions: Self-defense or defense of others against imminent unlawful force, within the limits of CRS 18-1-704

plain_language_summary: Door security cannot legally confiscate a patron's ID, including a suspected fake ID. The proper response to a suspected fake ID is to refuse entry, document the encounter, and call law enforcement if appropriate. Holding an ID against the patron's will may expose the venue to civil claims of theft, false imprisonment, or conversion.
required_actions: Refuse entry
Hand the ID back
Document the description and any photographs the patron consents to
Notify the manager
Call law enforcement if the patron behaves disorderly
prohibited_actions: Keeping the ID
Tearing or cutting the ID
Photographing the ID without consent
Detaining the patron to wait for police
allowed_actions: Refusing entry
Trespassing the patron from the premises through proper procedure

source.title: Colorado common law on conversion and false imprisonment; venue insurance carrier guidance

notes: Verify with legal counsel before treating as authoritative.

plain_language_summary: Security generally cannot detain patrons. A citizen's arrest (CRS 18-1-707) is high-risk and requires a felony or specified misdemeanor committed in the person's presence. Detention should be avoided unless law enforcement is en route for a serious immediate threat.
required_actions: Train staff to prioritize de-escalation and ejection over detention
Call 911 immediately if a serious crime is suspected
Document any brief detention and the immediate arrival of police
prohibited_actions: Holding a patron against their will for minor infractions or payment disputes
Using handcuffs without specific board-approved training and policy
allowed_actions: Requesting a patron stay voluntarily for an incident report
Blocking a path briefly to prevent a flight from a serious crime scene
exceptions: Citizen's arrest under CRS 18-1-707 with full understanding of the legal exposure

plain_language_summary: Bag checks at entry are generally permissible as a condition of entry on private property if the patron consents. Searching a patron's person (pockets, clothing) is high-risk and not condoned by house policy. Refused-search patrons may be denied entry but cannot be searched, restrained, or pursued.
required_actions: Post a sign at entry stating the bag-check policy
Use clear, calm language requesting to look inside the bag
Allow the patron to refuse and leave
prohibited_actions: Searching pockets or clothing
Touching the patron during a bag inspection
Confiscating items unless contraband is in plain view and law enforcement is being called
allowed_actions: Visual bag inspection at entry
Refusing entry on declined consent
Calling law enforcement if a weapon or controlled substance is observed in plain view

source.title: Colorado common law on private-property entry conditions

plain_language_summary: House policy prohibits door security from carrying firearms, knives, batons, tasers, pepper spray, or restraints while on duty. Colorado licensees in many jurisdictions face administrative penalties for armed unlicensed security on premises. Concealed-carry by patrons is a separate matter; house policy can prohibit it as a condition of entry with posted notice.
required_actions: Document the weapons-prohibited house policy
Post entry signage prohibiting patron weapons
Train staff to call law enforcement if a weapon is observed
prohibited_actions: Carrying any weapon while on door-security duty
exceptions: Licensed armed security personnel under contract with their own license, insurance, and training documentation

source.title: House policy; local jurisdiction private-security licensing where applicable

plain_language_summary: Maintain visible, calm presence near the entry or problem area without crowding guests. Visibility deters problems before they require intervention.
required_actions: Position yourself where most of the room can see you
Stay calm and unhurried
Avoid stopping in tight clusters with other staff
prohibited_actions: Looming over patrons
Cornering patrons in tight spaces
Public posturing or intimidation
allowed_actions: Walking the floor
Pausing at problem zones to observe

plain_language_summary: First contact with a guest of concern is verbal, calm, and at conversational distance. Lower the volume rather than raise it. Use the guest's first name if known.
required_actions: Approach at conversational distance, not arm's length
Lower your voice
Use simple direct phrasing
prohibited_actions: Yelling
Public accusations
Profanity
allowed_actions: Asking the guest to step aside for a private check-in
Inviting the guest outside for a calmer conversation

plain_language_summary: Stand at a slight angle to the guest, hands visible at waist or chest, weight balanced. This posture reads as ready but not aggressive and protects the centerline if a strike comes.
required_actions: Stand at 30-45 degree angle
Keep hands visible
Keep weight balanced on both feet
prohibited_actions: Squared-up posture with clenched fists
Hands hidden behind back
Crowding the guest
allowed_actions: Adjusting position to maintain exit awareness

plain_language_summary: Always preserve a clear path to an exit for both yourself and the guest. A patron who feels cornered escalates faster.
required_actions: Position yourself off-axis from the exit
Avoid backing the guest into a wall or corner
Verify the exit path is unblocked before initiating contact
prohibited_actions: Standing between the guest and the exit
Allowing other staff to block the exit
allowed_actions: Calling on radio for path clearance

plain_language_summary: Any service refusal, removal, or escalating disturbance is a manager event. Handoff transfers context — what was observed, what was said, what was tried — without rehashing accusations in front of the guest.
required_actions: Call the manager via radio
Brief the manager out of guest earshot
Stay nearby for support but step back from active conversation
prohibited_actions: Talking over the manager
Re-engaging the guest while the manager is speaking
allowed_actions: Documenting the handoff in the incident log

plain_language_summary: Standard flashlight for low-light visibility, attention cues, and ID inspection. Beam should never be aimed directly into a patron's eyes as a warning, punishment, or shaming gesture.
required_actions: Aim the beam at the floor, walls, or surfaces near the area of interest
Use brief flashes for attention cues per house signal protocol
prohibited_actions: Shining into a patron's eyes
Using as a weapon or impact tool
Striking with the flashlight
allowed_actions: Brief safety lighting toward eyes only when the patron is at risk of falling, e.g., on stairs

plain_language_summary: UV/blacklight flashlight for ID inspection. Many state IDs include UV-reactive features that are visible only under UV. Use to verify, not to dramatize.
required_actions: Use at the door under controlled lighting
Compare against the published UV feature for the issuing state
Decline entry if features fail or appear missing
prohibited_actions: Using UV beam toward eyes
Using as a generic flashlight

plain_language_summary: Two-way radio for staff-to-staff communication during service. All radio communication is per the calm-phrase protocol — no insults, accusations, or panic language.
required_actions: Test radio at start of shift
Use neutral phrases per the radio protocol
Confirm receipt with brief acknowledgement
prohibited_actions: Insults or accusations on radio
Joking during incidents
Patron names without operational need
allowed_actions: Brief codewords from the approved list

plain_language_summary: Door security may not carry the following on duty without owner + legal counsel + insurance carrier approval: firearms, knives, expandable batons, tasers, stun guns, pepper spray, mace, tactical gloves, handcuffs, restraints, or improvised weapons.
required_actions: Pre-shift bag check by manager
Lock personal weapons off premises
Document any incident involving prohibited equipment
prohibited_actions: Carrying any of the listed items on duty
exceptions: Licensed armed security under contract with their own license, insurance, and documented training

plain_language_summary: "Manager to front, please." — Manager requested at the door without announcing conflict details on radio.
required_actions: Use when door situation needs manager review
Wait for manager acknowledgement
prohibited_actions: Adding accusatory detail on radio
allowed_actions: Following up with quiet face-to-face context after manager arrives

plain_language_summary: "Can I get a floor check near [location]?" — Calm request for staff eyes on a specific area without naming patrons or alleging behavior.
required_actions: Use specific location, not patron description
prohibited_actions: Describing patrons by protected-class characteristics

plain_language_summary: "Water and food support at [location]." — Calm request to deliver water and food to a guest the staff is concerned about, before initiating a service refusal.
required_actions: Deliver water + food without commentary
Notify manager
prohibited_actions: Lecturing the guest
allowed_actions: Offering a non-alcoholic alternative

plain_language_summary: "Call EMS." — Direct, unambiguous request for medical response. No qualifications. Specific location follows immediately.
required_actions: Call 911
State location and observed condition
Stay with the patron until EMS arrives
prohibited_actions: Diagnosing the patron on radio
Delaying the call to consult management

plain_language_summary: One brief flash directed at the floor near the area of interest = staff attention requested at this location. Low-priority signal; observe and check in.
required_actions: Aim flash at floor, not patrons
Use sparingly
prohibited_actions: Flashing toward eyes
Flashing as discipline or shaming

plain_language_summary: Two brief flashes directed at the floor near the area of interest = manager or floor support requested. Medium-priority signal.
required_actions: Pair with radio call when possible
Wait for visual or radio acknowledgement
prohibited_actions: Flashing toward eyes

plain_language_summary: Repeated downward flashes directed at the floor near the area of interest = urgent support needed. Pair with radio call. High-priority signal.
required_actions: Pair with radio call
Move toward the support location once acknowledged
prohibited_actions: Flashing toward eyes
Substituting the flash for a verbal call

plain_language_summary: For one verbally upset patron with no immediate safety concern: manager + 1 trained support staff member preferred. Goal is witnessed de-escalation.
required_actions: Manager leads conversation
Support staff observes from non-confrontational distance
Document outcome in incident log
prohibited_actions: Crowding
Multiple staff talking simultaneously
Surrounding the patron
allowed_actions: Inviting the patron to a quieter area

plain_language_summary: For one visibly intoxicated or unsteady patron: manager + 1 support staff. Offer water, food, and a ride alternative before initiating service refusal.
required_actions: Offer water and food
Offer a ride alternative
Document the offer and the patron's response
prohibited_actions: Forceful removal as first action
Public shaming
allowed_actions: Calling EMS if medical impairment is suspected

plain_language_summary: For an active fight: staff safety first. Call law enforcement and EMS. Do not enter the fight unless there is an immediate safety need within training and capability. Clear bystanders, secure exits, document witnesses.
required_actions: Call 911
Clear bystanders to safe distance
Secure the exit path
Document witnesses and time
prohibited_actions: Entering a fight without immediate safety need
Group pile-on
Taunting
Using prohibited equipment
allowed_actions: Defensive blocking to protect a third party from imminent harm, within training
Verbal commands to disperse

plain_language_summary: Blocking walkway, repeated loud disruption, ignoring staff direction, unsafe glassware handling, entering restricted staff areas — low-severity behaviors. Initial action: manager or floor security observation. Next action: calm verbal redirect.
required_actions: Observe
Calm verbal redirect
prohibited_actions: Physical contact
Public scolding
Refusal based on protected-class traits
allowed_actions: Inviting the patron to move to a different area
Asking a 21+ companion to assist
notes: Enforce based on behavior, not appearance, identity, or perceived social status.

plain_language_summary: Harassment of other guests or staff, suspected fake ID requiring manager review, visible intoxication requiring service review, repeated unwanted contact, aggressive verbal behavior, refusing to leave after refusal — medium-severity. Manager review and likely service refusal.
required_actions: Observe
Manager review
Service refusal if pattern continues
Documentation
prohibited_actions: Physical removal as first action
Public confrontation in front of other guests
allowed_actions: Quiet escort to the exit at lowest force needed
Inviting the patron to a private area for a manager conversation

plain_language_summary: Threats, pushing/shoving, active fight, property damage, suspected weapon, medical distress, unconsciousness, sexual harassment or assault allegation — high-severity. Call law enforcement and EMS. Manager and at least one trained support staff respond. Documentation is mandatory.
required_actions: Call 911
Manager + trained support staff respond
Clear bystanders
Document witnesses, times, and observed behavior
prohibited_actions: Forceful removal beyond immediate self-defense
Searching the patron
Confiscating items
Group pile-on
allowed_actions: Defensive action against imminent unlawful force, within training and CRS 18-1-704
Brief safe escort to the exit
